title: Long Live the Fame of Perunthalaivar Kamaraj!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's commemorative voice, historical sequence, quoted speech, dates, figures, schemes, and paragraph order. The Tamil reading copy below removes a duplicated OCR title fragment and corrects clear OCR character and word-boundary errors encountered during translation. The canonical source document remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Today is the 112th birth anniversary of Perunthalaivar Kamaraj. I believe that recalling a few facts about him in today's letter would be a fitting tribute from the Kazhagam.
Two years ago, a *Dinamalar* special correspondent published a report under the headline, "Emergency Order to Celebrate Education Development Day Leaves Teachers in Distress." It said:

"During the previous DMK government, 15 July, Kamaraj's birthday, was declared Education Development Day. Various competitions were held in advance in every school, prizes were presented to the winners on 15 July, and the day was celebrated as a festival. Separate financial assistance was provided to government and government-aided schools. To ensure that the celebration continued, the previous DMK government enacted and implemented a separate law.

"Kamaraj's birthday is being observed today. But it is said that the Tamil Nadu government gave schools no advance announcement about celebrating the event or conducting competitions and awarding prizes.

"Only yesterday evening, district education officers and head teachers were hurriedly informed by fax and email that Kamaraj's birthday must be celebrated in every type of school, and that expenditure would later be reimbursed to government and government-aided schools. Teachers are shocked and do not know how to organise the event after this sudden last-minute order."
That was the report in *Dinamalar*.
Why did the government make such a sudden, hurried announcement only one day before Kamaraj's birthday? In a statement on 14 July 2011, I said:

"Beginning in 2007-08, the DMK government allocated separate funds to every school, announced that Perunthalaivar Kamaraj's birthday must be celebrated in each school as Education Development Day, and enacted it as law. Yet this year's government announcement merely advertises that ministers and Members of Parliament and the Legislative Assembly will garland Kamaraj's statue on his 109th birthday. It does not even identify the day as Education Development Day."

Only after this did the government wake up and decide to celebrate Kamaraj's birthday. That is why I waited until today to see what this government would do this year. The half-page government advertisement published today says that the Chief Minister and other ministers will offer flowers at Kamaraj's statue on Kamarajar Salai along the Marina. But it contains no instruction to schools to celebrate his birthday as Education Development Day.

The DMK government announced that Kamaraj's birthday would be celebrated as Education Development Day. Suspecting that a future government might obstruct the announcement's permanent implementation, we made it a law. The Legislative Assembly enacted a statute requiring schools to celebrate Kamaraj's birthday on 15 July, and the Kazhagam government secured it in law.

Someone may ask whether a law cannot be changed. It can. But changing an announcement is easy: it can be ignored until it disappears, or deliberately revoked by another announcement. A law, however, can be withdrawn or amended only by convening the Assembly. The government would have to assemble the legislature and openly say: "When Karunanidhi was Chief Minister during the Kazhagam government, he designated Kamaraj's birthday as Education Development Day, celebrated it and enacted a law. We are now withdrawing and repealing that law." Whether anyone could repeal such a law and still walk freely among the public is another matter. That is why we made it a statute.

The DMK's manifesto for the 2006 Assembly election promised: "We will declare 15 July, Kamaraj's birthday, as the day on which the eyes of education were opened, and celebrate it in schools." Accordingly, the Kazhagam government declared 15 July, the birthday of Perunthalaivar Kamaraj who opened Tamil Nadu's eyes to education, as Education Development Day. A Government Order was issued on 24 May 2006 and the measure was later enacted as law.

On 15 July 2006, Kamaraj's portrait was decorated with colourful flowers in schools across Tamil Nadu. Speech, essay and drawing competitions and cultural programmes were conducted, and prizes were awarded. At that celebration, a scheme providing eggs twice a week with the nutritious-meal programme to children aged two to fifteen and to school students was also launched across Tamil Nadu.

When Education Development Day was celebrated on 15 July 2007, the scheme was expanded to three eggs a week. On 15 July 2008, a scheme providing three bananas a week with the nutritious meal to children who did not eat eggs was introduced. These schemes were successfully implemented throughout the Kazhagam government's tenure.

Let me recall a few occasions on which the DMK honoured Perunthalaivar Kamaraj. When Kamaraj became Chief Minister, he was not a member of the Legislative Assembly. He had to be elected to either the Assembly or the Legislative Council within six months, and therefore contested the Gudiyatham by-election. Perarignar Anna wrote "O Man of Virtue, Scion of a Noble Line" in *Dravida Nadu*, declared that we would work for Kamaraj's victory, and extended his support. The DMK accordingly worked for Kamaraj and helped him win.
When the DMK administered the Madras Corporation, the Kazhagam erected a statue of Kamaraj near Periyar Bridge and had Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru unveil it on 9 October 1961.
After Perunthalaivar Kamaraj died on 2 October 1975, a memorial hall was built for him near the Gandhi Mandapam at Guindy, with a bust of Kamaraj unveiled at its entrance.
In 1990, the domestic terminal at Chennai airport was named the Kamaraj Terminal. In 1997, Chennai's beach road was named Kamarajar Salai.

Because construction near the shore was prohibited, it had not been possible to build a memorial at Kanyakumari where Kamaraj's ashes had been placed. I spoke to then Prime Minister Vajpayee, made a request and secured permission. The Kamaraj Mani Mandapam was completed and inaugurated on 2 October 2000.

In 2006, the government instituted an award in Kamaraj's name. Each year, an eminent person distinguished in public and social service was selected and presented with the Perunthalaivar Kamaraj Award and a cash prize of one lakh rupees.
Also in 2006, we enacted 15 July as Education Development Day and provided a separate annual allocation to every school so that the celebration could be conducted properly.
The Radhapuram bus stand in Tirunelveli district, built at a cost of 77 lakh rupees, was named after Perunthalaivar Kamaraj on 19 July 2010.

When the Emergency was imposed in India and leaders languished in prison, Kamaraj could not bear that democracy was collapsing in independent India and that his national colleagues were imprisoned. His health failed and he became bedridden.

One morning, Navalar, who was then Education Minister, and I went to Kamaraj's home. When he saw me sit beside him, his eyes filled with tears. I touched him gently. He embraced me, and his eyes became waterfalls. "The nation is lost! The nation is lost!" he cried aloud. I broke down and wept. Navalar, tears streaming from his own eyes, consoled us both.
I said: "Ayya, you give the word. We will all resign immediately. To restore democracy in India and destroy dictatorship, you take the lead and conduct the campaign. We are ready to follow you."
He advised: "Be patient. Do not act hastily. Today, Tamil Nadu is the only place in India where democracy still exists. If you resign, that too will be lost. Be patient for a little while."

Today is the 112th birth anniversary of such a Perunthalaivar. Whatever differences of policy or opinion we may have had with departed leaders and eminent persons, we have never forgotten their virtues, noble conduct, extraordinary public service, sacrifices and abilities. We continue to honour them without forgetting.

When the Kazhagam was in office, before every decision on matters such as the Cauvery dispute and the Emergency, I did not think of Kamaraj merely as a member of the opposition. I approached him as a senior and experienced political leader, went to his home, learnt his views and acted accordingly.
At the Second World Tamil Conference conducted while Perarignar Anna was Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu, it was Kamaraj who welcomed all those attending and inaugurated the conference.
How many such memories there are!

We must learn to respect our opponents. At evening public meetings, we may attack one another in many ways. After Congress rule in Tamil Nadu, the DMK came to power. Yet Perunthalaivar Kamaraj, who might have been expected to show resentment towards the DMK, never behaved that way. That is why he became Perunthalaivar. Tamil Nadu knows equally well the respect Perarignar Anna, and later I, showed Kamaraj and the relationship we maintained with him.
On this anniversary of his birth, I am happy to offer him my tribute of praise. Long live the fame of Perunthalaivar Kamaraj!
With affection,
M. K.
16 July 2014
